abstract:
Ultrafine tuning of metal-organic framework (MOF) structures at the sub-angstrom or picometer scale is a powerful method for enhancing separation selectivity between gas molecules with nearly identical dimensions. However, in MOFs with relatively large pores, the impact of such subtle structural adjustments is often negligible. In this work, we present an integrative strategy that couples extreme pore compression with ultrafine tuning to overcome this limitation. This approach leverages the unique characteristics of the partitioned *acs* (*pacs*) platform: a multimodular framework and an exceptional capacity for isoreticular replacement. 

We utilize a primary module (Ligand 1, L1) to drastically shrink the *pacs* pore size to its minimum; specifically, by replacing prototypical 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate with *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate, we achieved an extreme compression ratio of approximately 30% (based on the unit cell *c*/*a* ratio). This tightened environment then amplifies the effects of a second module (Ligand 2, L2), which provides ultrafine pore tuning at a scale of less than 3% compression. This dual-ligand strategy dramatically boosts C₂H₂/CO₂ selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8, leading to superior experimental breakthrough performance. Furthermore, by introducing *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate—the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate used as a bioisosteric mimic of para-benzene moieties—this study opens new avenues for structural engineering in MOF chemistry.
